main_ingredients: kadhai masala (coriander, cumin, red chilli, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Fish Kadhai is approximately 350 g (1 bowl), as commonly portioned at Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Fish curry (estimated from fish curry composition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Fish, mixed species, cooked (ID 15264) with tomato-cream gravy adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source contains an exact entry for Fish Kadhai as a restaurant-prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN fish curry composition and USDA FoodData Central cooked fish data, adjusted for the tomato-cream-spice gravy profile. Input values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against these approved sources and retained where consistent. Values are plausible for a moderate-fat fish curry with cream.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on fish curry / mixed vegetable curry with tomato base (GI ~35)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Fish Kadhai. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous fish curry and tomato-vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The low GI reflects the dominant protein and fat content from fish and cream, low net carbohydrate (7.14 g/100g), and the presence of fibre-rich vegetables and spices. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 24.99) / 100 ≈ 8.7 (350 g serving × 7.14 g carbohydrate/100g = 24.99 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 271 mg/100g (949 mg per 350 g serving) approaches the upper daily limit for hypertensive individuals; cream and butter contribute saturated fat which can elevate blood pressure over time.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt and cream in preparation, limit portion to 200 g, and pair with potassium-rich foods such as leafy greens or legumes to offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low net carbohydrate (7.14 g/100g) with high protein (10 g/100g) from fish slow postprandial glucose rise; fenugreek leaves (kasoori methi) have evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fish Kadhai is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard 350 g portion; cream and butter should be minimised to reduce saturated fat intake that may worsen insulin resistance, and the dish should be paired with a low-GI carbohydrate such as basmati rice or roti in controlled quantities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cream and butter (1.71 g/100g; ~6 g per serving) and sodium (271 mg/100g; ~949 mg per serving) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; fish provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with minimal butter and cream or substitution with low-fat yogurt, limit sodium by reducing added salt, and benefit from the omega-3 content of the fish component which supports favourable lipid profiles.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic, fenugreek, coriander) and omega-3-rich fish provide meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit; nightshade vegetables (tomato, capsicum, chilli) may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fish Kadhai is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory spice profile and fish omega-3 content; those with nightshade sensitivity should request reduced tomato and capsicum, and cream should be replaced with low-fat yogurt to reduce saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Kadhai (also known as Kadhai Fish or Karahi Fish) is a popular North Indian fish curry prepared in a kadhai (Indian wok), a heavy-bottomed round-bottomed cooking vessel that imparts a characteristic smoky, concentrated flavour. The dish features firm white fish fillets simmered in a rich, spiced tomato-onion gravy enriched with cream and butter, and seasoned with kadhai masala — a blend of coriander seeds, cumin, dried red chillies, and garam masala. Aromatic additions include ginger, garlic, capsicum (bell pepper), fenugreek leaves (kasoori methi), and turmeric, which together create a deeply fragrant, mildly spiced, and slightly tangy sauce. The fish is typically added towards the end of cooking to prevent overcooking, resulting in tender, flaky pieces coated in the thick, glossy gravy. The texture is creamy and rich, with a balance of heat from chillies, earthiness from coriander and cumin, and brightness from tomatoes. Nutritionally, Fish Kadhai provides approximately 137 kcal per 100 g, with 10 g protein, 8 g fat, and 7.1 g carbohydrate. The low glycemic index (~35) and high protein content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium at 271 mg/100g warrants moderation for hypertensive individuals. The omega-3 fatty acids from fish and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) provide cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits. In Singapore, Fish Kadhai is served at Indian restaurants as a main course, typically accompanied by naan, roti, or steamed basmati rice, and is popular across the Indian community and among food enthusiasts seeking bold, aromatic Indian flavours.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Fish Kadhai?
Is Fish Kadhai good for diabetes?
Is Kadhai Fish high in sodium?
What spices are in Fish Kadhai?
What is the difference between Fish Kadhai and Fish Curry?
Can I eat Fish Kadhai if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry exists for Fish Kadhai as a restaurant-prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN fish curry composition and USDA FoodData Central cooked fish data, cross-checked against the ai_generated input values (confidence 0.7). The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence reflects the absence of a direct approved-source match and the composite nature of the dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Fish Kadhai as a composite restaurant-prepared dish are not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. trans_fat_g retained at 0.03 g/100g from input (trace level from butter/cream component; consistent with dairy-derived trans fat at low levels in cream-enriched dishes). The input source listed 'https://foodo.sg/' as the HPB Singapore URL, which is not the official HPB nutrient database URL; this source was not used as primary nutrient evidence. Nutrient values were cross-validated against ICMR-NIN and USDA approved sources. The ethnic_tag 'Butter Chicken' in the input has been removed as it is not applicable to Fish Kadhai. Multiple portions in the input all listed 350 g with is_default: true; only '1 bowl' at 350 g is retained as the default serving.
